channel.title: Adeniyi Adebayo
channel.description: Writing on cities, marketplaces, and emerging markets.

item.title: Mr. Fabiyi
item.description: The Further Maths teacher at Sharon Rose who assigned more than anyone could finish, marked what was there, and kept his one promise at WAEC.

item.title: The Boy from Saki
item.description: A town near the Benin border, the factories that cracked the world open, a notice board at Ile-Ife, and the conviction that capability is learnable and self-doubt is fuel.

item.title: Rails Before Marketplaces
item.description: Marketplaces of convenience cannot come before marketplaces of necessity. In emerging markets infrastructure leapfrogs but behavior does not, and that fixes the order you can build in.

item.title: The Fiftieth User
item.description: Urbanization is not only happening in capitals. Why secondary cities are where the people already are, and why capital that crowds the top four buys the same user fifty times.

item.title: The Identity Premium
item.description: Identity is cheap in emerging markets because it does not compound. Why static credit snapshots fail, and how owning the transaction pipeline turns daily flow into an asset.

item.title: The Distribution Stack
item.description: Distribution in Africa runs on three layers: social discovery, trust nodes, and agent density. Wave and Moniepoint show what happens when you get the sequence right.

item.title: Inflation's Uneven Tax
item.description: A 2014 Toyota in Accra held its dollar value while cedi savers lost forty percent. The ladder of productive assets in soft-currency economies, and who actually pays inflation's tax.

item.title: No Float Expected
item.description: Cash, agents, and the price of delay. Why gig marketplaces in emerging markets must pay daily — and what agent networks in Kenya and Senegal reveal about the cost of settlement lag.

item.title: Pricing as Language
item.description: A price is a sentence in the language of an economy. Most companies entering emerging markets get the grammar wrong because they translate from somewhere else.

item.title: The Second Player Problem
item.description: Why second movers almost never catch density leaders in marketplace businesses. The race is won by having a density strategy when you enter a city, not by entering first.

item.title: What We Get Wrong About Cities
item.description: City GDP is the real signal, not country GDP. Why venture capital in emerging markets is misallocated by population thinking, and what Abidjan reveals about the actual math.

item.title: The Density Dividend
item.description: Market density in emerging market cities follows power law dynamics. Why Geoffrey West's scaling laws, Glaeser's agglomeration economics, and Jane Jacobs's street-level conditions explain where marketplace businesses actually compound.

item.title: The Informal Stack
item.description: The informal economy runs on its own logic. De Soto's dead capital, Scott's metis, and what marketplace platforms actually build on top of $9.3 trillion in unrecognized assets.

item.title: The Leapfrog That Isn't
item.description: Physical AI in emerging markets won't follow the mobile phone playbook. Why labor cost thresholds, city structure, and demographic scale determine what gets skipped and what doesn't.

item.title: Entropy and Marketplaces
item.description: Why most marketplace disruption destroys the resources it needs to sustain itself, and how to design for reversible entropy.

item.title: The First 72 Hours in a New City
item.description: The checklist for assessing a marketplace opportunity on the ground. Three things matter in the first 72 hours.

item.title: The Financing Loop
item.description: How vehicles, data, and banks create a self-reinforcing system in emerging market transport.

item.title: Boring Businesses That Print Money
item.description: Pharmacies, BNPL, and the economics of institutional vacuums in emerging markets.

item.title: Addis Ababa
item.description: The city most people in emerging market tech are missing. Four things converging.

item.title: Know Thyself
item.description: Market density, monopoly thinking, and the math of venture ambition.
